questions.question: Describe the steps taken by Akbar to expand his empire. Which territories did he conquer and when?
questions.answer: Akbar adopted two main policies for expansion: direct conquest and matrimonial alliances. His first expedition in 1559-60 captured Gwalior, followed by Malwa where Baz Bahadur was defeated and fled to Burhanpur. In 1564, he conquered Gondwana, where Rani Durgawati's kingdom was annexed. The conquest of Gujarat in 1572 was particularly significant because of its rich cotton industry, seaports, and thriving trade. Between 1574 and 1576, Bengal was brought under Mughal control, and in 1592, Orissa was annexed. In the north-west, Akbar conquered Kabul, Kashmir, Qandahar, Lower Sind, and eastern Baluchistan. His Deccan campaign began in 1590, when he attacked Ahmadnagar, which was bravely defended by Chand Bibi, though only part was annexed. In 1601, he invaded Khandesh and captured the forts of Burhanpur and Asirgarh. Through these conquests, Akbar transformed the Mughal empire from a vulnerable state into one of the largest empires in Indian history.

questions.question: Explain the religious policy of Akbar in detail.
questions.answer: Akbar held liberal religious views and believed in the basic unity of all religions. He married a Rajput princess and allowed her to practise her own religion freely. In 1562, he took the significant step of abolishing both the pilgrim tax and jizya on Hindus, winning their goodwill. He actively participated in Hindu festivals like Diwali, Holi, and Raksha Bandhan, and abolished the forceful conversion of prisoners of war. In 1575, he built Fatehpur Sikri as his new capital and invited religious scholars from different faiths, including Hindus, Jains, Parsis, and Christians, for discussions. After these discussions, he suggested Din-i-Ilahi (religion of one God), which was not a new religion but was like a Sufi order. Under Din-i-Ilahi, Akbar became the spiritual guide. He discouraged the killing of animals, disapproved of Sati, and introduced the worship of Sun and Fire in rituals. He built the Ibadat Khana (Hall of Worship) for these discussions. Importantly, he did not force anyone to accept Din-i-Ilahi. Even his trusted minister Raja Man Singh refused, while only Raja Birbal followed it. After Akbar's death in 1605, Din-i-Ilahi faded out, though his policy of secularism was continued by his son Jahangir.

questions.question: Describe the central administrative system during Akbar's reign.
questions.answer: Akbar's central administration, documented by Abul Fazl in the Ain-i-Akbari, was a sophisticated mix of Central Asian and Indian traditions. The Emperor was the supreme head who controlled all political, military, and judicial powers. All officers owed their power and position directly to the Emperor. Several departments were created for efficient governance, and official meetings were held in the Diwan-i-Khas where nobles and department heads reported to the Emperor. The key officials in the central administration were: the Wazir or Diwan, who was the head of the revenue department and managed the finances; the Mir Bakshi, who handled military administration and maintenance of armed contingents; the Mir Saman, who was the officer in-charge of royal Karkhanas (manufacturing units); the Sadr-us-Sudur, who protected the laws of Islam; and the Qazi, who was the head of the judiciary. For army administration, Akbar introduced the Mansabdari system where every officer was a Mansabdar with ranks denoted by Zat and Sawar. He also maintained string artillery and ahadis (special cavalry), creating a highly organised military force.

questions.question: Explain the revenue system under Akbar's reign in detail.
questions.answer: Under Akbar, land and trade were the two main sources of income for the empire. Raja Todar Mal was placed in charge of land revenue and assessment and developed the revenue system known as dahsaal or zabti system. One of his important innovations was the introduction of bamboo sticks called jarib with iron rings at the ends for measuring land, replacing the traditional rope which would expand or contract depending on wet or dry conditions. Land was classified into four categories: Polaj (land under cultivation), Parauti (land left fallow to regain fertility), Chachar (land not cultivated for 3-5 years), and Banjar (land not cultivated for more than 5 years). The dahsaal system, introduced in 1580, fixed the revenue amount by assessing the average produce and prices of the last 10 years, making it fair and predictable. If crops failed due to drought or floods, the government remitted the value, showing concern for farmers. Akbar also encouraged internal and external trade, with Gujarat and Bengal serving as major centres of foreign trade. The currency consisted of gold coins called mohur, silver coins called rupee, and copper coins called dam or paisa.

questions.question: Write about the social and cultural contributions of Akbar, covering literature, music, painting, and architecture.
questions.answer: Akbar's reign saw remarkable social and cultural achievements. In literature, although Akbar himself never learnt to read or write, he was fond of knowledge and had books read to him. His court had two literary gems: Abul Fazl, who wrote the Akbarnama (a biographical account that took seven years), part of which was the Ain-i-Akbari; and Faizi, a Persian poet. Akbar got the Ramayana and Mahabharata translated from Sanskrit to Persian. Hindi poetry also flourished with Keshav, Rahim, Surdas, and Tulsidas, whose Ramcharitmanas is still read worldwide. In music, Akbar had 36 musicians in his court. Tansen was the most famous, composing Raag Darbari and Deepak Raag and introducing Persian elements into Indian music. In painting, a new style emerged mixing Indian and Persian traditions, featuring red and blue colours and miniature painting. Jaswant and Daswana were famous painters, and about 250 paintings were commissioned. In architecture, Akbar built Fatehpur Sikri with its blend of Islamic and Hindu elements in red sandstone with white marble, including the famous Buland Darwaza (highest gateway in India), Tomb of Sheikh Salim Chisti, Panch Mahal, and Jodha Bai's Palace. Humayun's Tomb in New Delhi was also built during this period.

questions.question: Discuss Akbar's relations with the Rajputs and the significance of the Battle of Haldighati.
questions.answer: Akbar adopted a remarkably progressive policy towards the Rajputs, based on respect, alliance, and inclusion rather than suppression. He showed great reverence and inducted Rajputs into Mughal service through matrimonial alliances, beginning with Raja Bharmal of Amber, who was the first Rajput to enter such an alliance. The Rajput kingdoms of Merta and Jodhpur were subjugated without much resistance, and by 1570, almost the whole of Rajasthan was under Mughal control without hostility. Akbar never interfered in the internal matters of the Rajputs and offered them high administrative posts, with Raja Birbal and Raja Man Singh being his most trusted Rajput officials. Ranthambhor, Kalinjar were captured, and Marwar, Bikaner, and Jaisalmer also submitted. However, not all Rajput chiefs accepted Mughal authority. Udai Singh of Mewar fought against Akbar and was defeated, but his son Maharana Pratap continued the resistance. The Battle of Haldighati on 18 June 1576 was fought between Maharana Pratap's forces and the Mughal army led by Man Singh I of Amber. Maharana Pratap was defeated and took shelter in a mountain fortress. Although a large number of Rajput soldiers were killed, resistance from Mewar continued, and in 1597, Maharana Pratap recovered part of his dominion.

questions.question: Explain the features of the Mansabdari system and provincial administration under Akbar.
questions.answer: The Mansabdari system was a unique military-administrative system introduced by Akbar. The word 'Mansab' literally means office or rank, and every officer or noble was called a Mansabdar. Each mansabdar's rank was expressed through two numbers: Zat (personal rank determining position in the hierarchy) and Sawar (the size of the cavalry contingent to be maintained). Zat ranks ranged from 10 to 10,000, with ranks above 50,000 reserved exclusively for princes. Mansabdars were the highest paid officers, given handsome jagirs for personal expenditure and to maintain horses, elephants, and camels. They could be paid in cash or through jagirs assessed to match their salary. A crucial feature was that rank was NOT hereditary - it was held only during the mansabdar's lifetime, and after death, the jagir reverted to the emperor. This prevented the growth of a feudal class. Akbar also maintained string artillery and ahadis (special cavalry). For provincial administration, the empire was divided into twelve subas, each under a Subedar directly appointed by the Emperor. Subas were divided into sarkars (districts), then parganas, with the village under a panchayat as the lowest unit. The Sadr handled welfare and judicial matters at the provincial level, while the Kotwal managed town administration and police duties.

questions.question: Describe the condition of literature during Akbar's rule and the contributions of major literary figures.
questions.answer: Literature flourished magnificently under Akbar's patronage despite the fact that Akbar himself never learnt to read or write. He was deeply fond of knowledge and had books read to him regularly. His court had two great literary gems: Abul Fazl and Faizi. Abul Fazl wrote the Akbarnama, which was the official biographical account of Akbar's life commissioned by the emperor himself. This monumental work took seven years to complete and contained numerous paintings. A part of the Akbarnama was the Ain-i-Akbari, which documented the law and revenue system of the empire. Faizi was an accomplished poet who wrote in Persian and enriched the court's literary culture. Akbar promoted cultural exchange by getting the great Hindu epics - the Ramayana and the Mahabharata - translated from Sanskrit to Persian, making them accessible to a wider audience. Hindi poetry also flourished during this period, with major poets including Keshav, Rahim (Abdul Rahim Khan-i-Khana, son of Bairam Khan), Surdas, and Tulsidas. Tulsidas composed the Ramcharitmanas during this era, a work that continues to be read with reverence across the world, demonstrating the lasting literary legacy of Akbar's reign.
